key_insight: Machine-learning-accelerated path-integral simulations show that nuclear quantum effects strongly enhance hydrogen diffusion through layered MoS2 and create a clear hydrogen–deuterium isotope barrier difference.
engineering_relevance: These results matter for designing 2D-material membranes and interfaces for hydrogen storage, isotope separation, and fuel-cell transport where quantum nuclear motion can change performance predictions.
method: Train interatomic potentials on r2SCAN+rVV10 DFT data
Combine well-tempered metadynamics with path-integral molecular dynamics
Compare hydrogen and deuterium diffusion across MoS2 polytypes and twisted bilayers
evidence_signal: The abstract reports lower quantum free-energy barriers at 300 K, a larger hydrogen self-diffusion coefficient than in classical simulations, and a 35 meV H–D barrier difference.
watch_item: The conclusions depend on the quality and transferability of the learned potential across polytypes and moiré stacking environments, so out-of-distribution structures should be validated carefully.
paper_title: Capturing Nuclear Quantum Effects in Hydrogen Diffusion through MoS2 via Machine-Learning-Enhanced Path-Integral Simulations
